mn22:1.2: at one time the *Bhagavā* was dwelling at Sāvatthī, in Jeta’s Grove, Anāthapiṇḍika’s park.
mn22:10.1: Here, *bhikkhus*, some hollow men learn the *dhamma*—
mn22:10.10: Suppose, *bhikkhus*, a man needing a snake, seeking a snake, searching for a snake.
mn22:10.11: He might see a large snake.
mn22:10.12: He might grasp it by its coils or by its tail.
mn22:10.13: That snake, turning back on him, might bite his hand, arm, or some other limb.
mn22:10.14: Because of that he might meet death or death-like *dukkha*.
mn22:10.16: Because of the bad grasp of the snake, *bhikkhus*.
mn22:10.17: So too, *bhikkhus*, here some hollow men learn the *dhamma*—
mn22:10.18: *suttas*, songs, expositions, verses, inspired utterances, sayings, birth stories, marvels, and question-and-answer dialogues.
mn22:10.19: Having learned that *dhamma*, they do not examine the meaning of those *dhammas* with discernment [*paññāya*].
mn22:10.2: *suttas*, songs, expositions, verses, inspired utterances, sayings, birth stories, marvels, and question-and-answer dialogues.
mn22:10.20: Because they have not examined the meaning with discernment [*paññāya*], those *dhammas* do not become acceptable to them through reflection.
mn22:10.21: They learn the *dhamma* for the benefit of criticizing and for the benefit of escaping from disputes.
mn22:10.22: And they do not experience the purpose for which they learn the *dhamma*.
mn22:10.23: Those *dhammas*, badly grasped by them, lead to their harm and *dukkha* for a long time.
mn22:10.25: Because of the bad grasp of the *dhammas*, *bhikkhus*.
mn22:10.3: Having learned that *dhamma*, they do not examine the meaning of those *dhammas* with discernment [*paññāya*].
mn22:10.4: Because they have not examined the meaning with discernment [*paññāya*], those *dhammas* do not become acceptable to them through reflection.
mn22:10.5: They learn the *dhamma* for the benefit of criticizing and for the benefit of escaping from disputes.
mn22:10.6: And they do not experience the purpose for which they learn the *dhamma*.
mn22:10.7: Those *dhammas*, badly grasped by them, lead to their harm and *dukkha* for a long time.
mn22:10.9: Because of the bad grasp of the *dhammas*, *bhikkhus*.
mn22:11.1: But here, *bhikkhus*, some sons of good family learn the *dhamma*—
mn22:11.10: Suppose, *bhikkhus*, a man needing a snake, seeking a snake, searching for a snake.
mn22:11.11: He might see a large snake.
mn22:11.12: He would pin it down firmly with a forked stick.
mn22:11.13: Having pinned it down firmly with a forked stick, he would grasp it well by the neck.
mn22:11.14: Even if that snake were to wrap its coils around that man’s hand, arm, or some other limb, because of that he would not meet death or death-like *dukkha*.
mn22:11.16: Because of the good grasp of the snake, *bhikkhus*.
mn22:11.17: So too, *bhikkhus*, here some sons of good family learn the *dhamma*—
mn22:11.18: *suttas*, songs, expositions, verses, inspired utterances, sayings, birth stories, marvels, and question-and-answer dialogues.
mn22:11.19: Having learned that *dhamma*, they examine the meaning of those *dhammas* with discernment [*paññāya*].
mn22:11.2: *suttas*, songs, expositions, verses, inspired utterances, sayings, birth stories, marvels, and question-and-answer dialogues.
mn22:11.20: Because they have examined the meaning with discernment [*paññāya*], those *dhammas* become acceptable to them through reflection.
mn22:11.21: They do not learn the *dhamma* for the benefit of criticizing or for the benefit of escaping from disputes.
mn22:11.22: And they experience the purpose for which they learn the *dhamma*.
mn22:11.23: Those *dhammas*, well grasped by them, lead to their good, welfare, and *sukha* for a long time.
mn22:11.25: Because of the good grasp of the *dhammas*, *bhikkhus*.
mn22:11.3: Having learned that *dhamma*, they examine the meaning of those *dhammas* with discernment [*paññāya*].
mn22:11.4: Because they have examined the meaning with discernment [*paññāya*], those *dhammas* become acceptable to them through reflection.
mn22:11.5: They do not learn the *dhamma* for the benefit of criticizing or for the benefit of escaping from disputes.
mn22:11.6: And they experience the purpose for which they learn the *dhamma*.
mn22:11.7: Those *dhammas*, well grasped by them, lead to their welfare and *sukha* for a long time.
mn22:11.9: Because of the good grasp of the *dhammas*, *bhikkhus*.
mn22:12.1: Therefore, *bhikkhus*, when you understand the meaning of what I have said, you should remember it in that way.
mn22:12.2: But when you do not understand the meaning of what I have said, there you should question me or skilled *bhikkhus*.
mn22:13.1: *Bhikkhus*, I will teach you the *dhamma* with the simile of a raft, for the purpose of crossing over, not for the purpose of holding on.
mn22:13.10: but there is no boat for crossing over or bridge for going from the near shore to the far shore.
mn22:13.11: What if I gathered grass, sticks, branches, and leaves, bound together a raft, and, relying on that raft, striving with hands and feet, crossed safely to the far shore?’
mn22:13.12: Then, *bhikkhus*, that man, having gathered grass, sticks, branches, and leaves and bound together a raft, relying on that raft, striving with hands and feet, might cross safely to the far shore.
mn22:13.13: When that man had crossed and gone to the far shore, it might occur to him:
mn22:13.14: ‘This raft has been very helpful to me;
mn22:13.15: relying on this raft, striving with hands and feet, I have safely crossed to the far shore.
mn22:13.16: What if I hoisted this raft onto my head or lifted it onto my shoulder and then went wherever I wish?’
mn22:13.18: would that man, acting in this way, be doing what should be done with that raft?”
mn22:13.2: Listen to it, attend carefully, and I will speak.”
mn22:13.20: “And how, *bhikkhus*, would that man be doing what should be done with that raft?
mn22:13.21: Here, *bhikkhus*, when that man had crossed and gone to the far shore, it might occur to him:
mn22:13.22: ‘This raft has been very helpful to me;
mn22:13.23: relying on this raft, striving with hands and feet, I have safely crossed to the far shore.
mn22:13.24: What if I hauled this raft up on dry land or sank it in the water and then went wherever I wish?’
mn22:13.25: Acting in this way, *bhikkhus*, that man would be doing what should be done with that raft.
mn22:13.26: So too, *bhikkhus*, the *dhamma* has been taught by me with the simile of a raft, for the purpose of crossing over, not for the purpose of holding on.
mn22:13.5: “Suppose, *bhikkhus*, a man were traveling along a road.
mn22:13.6: He might see a great expanse of water, with this near shore dangerous and fearful, and the far shore secure and free from fear;
mn22:13.7: but there would be no boat for crossing over or bridge for going from the near shore to the far shore.
mn22:13.9: ‘This is a great expanse of water, with this near shore dangerous and fearful, and the far shore secure and free from fear;
mn22:14.1: *Bhikkhus*, knowing that the *dhamma* has been taught to you with the simile of a raft, even *dhammas* should be abandoned by you, how much more non-*dhammas*.
mn22:15.1: There are these six bases for views, *bhikkhus*.
mn22:15.10: ‘That is the world, that is the self; after death I will be that: permanent, lasting, eternal, not subject to change, I will stand just so for eternity’—
mn22:15.11: that too he regards thus: ‘This is mine, this I am, this is my self.’
mn22:15.3: Here, *bhikkhus*, an uninstructed ordinary person, who has not seen the noble ones, is unskilled in the noble *dhamma*, untrained in the noble *dhamma*, who has not seen true persons, is unskilled in the *dhamma* of true persons, untrained in the *dhamma* of true persons,
mn22:15.4: regards form [*rūpaṁ*] thus: ‘This is mine, this I am, this is my self’;
mn22:15.5: regards *vedanā* thus: ‘This is mine, this I am, this is my self’;
mn22:15.6: regards *saññā* thus: ‘This is mine, this I am, this is my self’;
mn22:15.7: regards *saṅkhāras* thus: ‘This is mine, this I am, this is my self’;
mn22:15.8: and whatever is seen, heard, sensed, cognized, reached, sought, and examined by the mind, that too he regards thus: ‘This is mine, this I am, this is my self’;
mn22:15.9: and also this basis for views—
mn22:16.1: But, *bhikkhus*, an instructed noble disciple, who has seen the noble ones, is skilled in the noble *dhamma*, well trained in the noble *dhamma*, who has seen true persons, is skilled in the *dhamma* of true persons, well trained in the *dhamma* of true persons,
mn22:16.2: regards form [*rūpaṁ*] thus: ‘This is not mine, this I am not, this is not my self’;
mn22:16.3: regards *vedanā* thus: ‘This is not mine, this I am not, this is not my self’;
mn22:16.4: regards *saññā* thus: ‘This is not mine, this I am not, this is not my self’;
mn22:16.5: regards *saṅkhāras* thus: ‘This is not mine, this I am not, this is not my self’;
mn22:16.6: and whatever is seen, heard, sensed, cognized, reached, sought, and examined by the mind, that too he regards thus: ‘This is not mine, this I am not, this is not my self’;
mn22:16.7: and also this basis for views—
mn22:16.8: ‘That is the world, that is the self; after death I will be that: permanent, lasting, eternal, not subject to change, I will stand just so for eternity’—
mn22:16.9: that too he regards thus: ‘This is not mine, this I am not, this is not my self.’
mn22:17.1: Seeing thus, when what is not exists, he is not agitated.”
mn22:18.1: When this was said, a certain *bhikkhu* said this to the *Bhagavā*:
mn22:18.2: “Could there be agitation, *bhante*, because of what is external not existing?”
mn22:18.5: ‘What was mine, alas, is no longer mine;
mn22:18.6: what could be mine, alas, I do not get.’
mn22:18.7: He sorrows, wearies, laments, beats his breast, cries, and falls into confusion.
mn22:18.8: Thus, *bhikkhu*, there is agitation because of what is external not existing.”
mn22:19.1: “But could there be non-agitation, *bhante*, because of what is external not existing?”
mn22:19.3: “Here, *bhikkhu*, it does not occur to someone:
mn22:19.4: ‘What was mine, alas, is no longer mine;
mn22:19.5: what could be mine, alas, I do not get.’
mn22:19.6: He does not sorrow, does not weary, does not lament, does not beat his breast, does not cry, and does not fall into confusion.
mn22:19.7: Thus, *bhikkhu*, there is non-agitation because of what is external not existing.”
mn22:2.1: Now at that time a harmful view of this kind had arisen in a *bhikkhu* named Ariṭṭha, formerly a vulture-trainer:
mn22:2.2: “As I understand the *dhamma* taught by the *Bhagavā*, those things called obstructions by the *Bhagavā* are not enough to obstruct one who engages in them.”
mn22:2.4: “It seems that a harmful view of this kind has arisen in a *bhikkhu* named Ariṭṭha, formerly a vulture-trainer:
mn22:2.5: ‘As I understand the *dhamma* taught by the *Bhagavā*, those things called obstructions by the *Bhagavā* are not enough to obstruct one who engages in them.’”
mn22:20.1: “Could there be agitation, *bhante*, because of what is internal not existing?”
mn22:20.4: ‘That is the world, that is the self; after death I will be that: permanent, lasting, eternal, not subject to change, I will stand just so for eternity.’
mn22:20.5: He hears the Tathāgata or a disciple of the Tathāgata teaching the *dhamma* for the uprooting of all grounds, adherences, obsessions, insistings, and underlying tendencies of views, for the settling [*samathāya*] of all *saṅkhāras*, for the relinquishment of all acquisitions, for the ending of *taṇhā*, for fading away, for cessation, for *nibbāna*.
mn22:20.7: ‘I will be cut off, it seems; I will perish, it seems; I will not exist, it seems.’
mn22:20.8: He sorrows, wearies, laments, beats his breast, cries, and falls into confusion.
mn22:20.9: Thus, *bhikkhu*, there is agitation because of what is internal not existing.”
mn22:21.1: “But could there be non-agitation, *bhante*, because of what is internal not existing?”
mn22:21.3: “Here, *bhikkhu*, someone does not have this view:
mn22:21.4: ‘That is the world, that is the self; after death I will be that: permanent, lasting, eternal, not subject to change, I will stand just so for eternity.’
mn22:21.5: He hears the Tathāgata or a disciple of the Tathāgata teaching the *dhamma* for the uprooting of all grounds, adherences, obsessions, insistings, and underlying tendencies of views, for the settling [*samathāya*] of all *saṅkhāras*, for the relinquishment of all acquisitions, for the ending of *taṇhā*, for fading away, for cessation, for *nibbāna*.
mn22:21.6: It does not occur to him:
mn22:21.7: ‘I will be cut off, it seems; I will perish, it seems; I will not exist, it seems.’
mn22:21.8: He does not sorrow, does not weary, does not lament, does not beat his breast, does not cry, and does not fall into confusion.
mn22:21.9: Thus, *bhikkhu*, there is non-agitation because of what is internal not existing.
mn22:22.1: *Bhikkhus*, you might take up an acquisition, if that acquisition were permanent, lasting, eternal, not subject to change, and would stand just so for eternity.
mn22:22.2: Do you see, *bhikkhus*, any acquisition that is permanent, lasting, eternal, not subject to change, and would stand just so for eternity?”
mn22:22.5: I too, *bhikkhus*, do not see any acquisition that is permanent, lasting, eternal, not subject to change, and would stand just so for eternity.
mn22:23.1: *Bhikkhus*, you might take up an *upādāna* of a doctrine of self, if, when taking up that *upādāna* of a doctrine of self, sorrow, lamentation, *dukkha*, dejection, and trouble would not arise.
mn22:23.2: Do you see, *bhikkhus*, any *upādāna* of a doctrine of self such that, when taking up that *upādāna* of a doctrine of self, sorrow, lamentation, *dukkha*, dejection, and trouble would not arise?”
mn22:23.5: I too, *bhikkhus*, do not see any *upādāna* of a doctrine of self such that, when taking up that *upādāna* of a doctrine of self, sorrow, lamentation, *dukkha*, dejection, and trouble would not arise.
mn22:24.1: *Bhikkhus*, you might rely on a support for views if, when relying on that support for views, sorrow, lamentation, *dukkha*, dejection, and trouble would not arise.
mn22:24.2: Do you see, *bhikkhus*, any support for views such that, when relying on that support for views, sorrow, lamentation, *dukkha*, dejection, and trouble would not arise?”
mn22:24.5: I too, *bhikkhus*, do not see any support for views such that, when relying on that support for views, sorrow, lamentation, *dukkha*, dejection, and trouble would not arise.
mn22:25.1: If there were self, *bhikkhus*, would there be ‘what belongs to my self’?”
mn22:25.3: “If there were what belongs to self, *bhikkhus*, would there be ‘my self’?”
mn22:25.5: “But when self and what belongs to self are not found as true and established, then this basis for views:
mn22:25.6: ‘That is the world, that is the self; after death I will be that: permanent, lasting, eternal, not subject to change, I will stand just so for eternity’—
mn22:25.7: is this not, *bhikkhus*, a wholly complete foolish *dhamma*?”
mn22:25.8: “How could it not be, *bhante*? It is indeed, *bhante*, a wholly complete foolish *dhamma*.”
mn22:26.15: “But what is impermanent [*aniccaṁ*] — is that *dukkha* or *sukha*?”
mn22:26.17: “But what is impermanent [*aniccaṁ*], *dukkha*, and subject to change — is it fitting to regard it thus:
mn22:26.18: ‘This is mine, this I am, this is my self’?”
mn22:26.4: “But what is impermanent [*aniccaṁ*] — is that *dukkha* or *sukha*?”
mn22:26.6: “But what is impermanent [*aniccaṁ*], *dukkha*, and subject to change — is it fitting to regard it thus:
mn22:26.7: ‘This is mine, this I am, this is my self’?”
mn22:27.1: “Therefore, *bhikkhus*, whatever form [*rūpaṁ*] there is — past, future, or present; internal or external; gross or subtle; inferior or superior; far or near — all form [*rūpaṁ*] should be seen as it truly is with right discernment [*sammappaññāya*] thus: ‘This is not mine, this I am not, this is not my self.’
mn22:27.5: whatever *viññāṇa* there is — past, future, or present; internal or external; gross or subtle; inferior or superior; far or near — all *viññāṇa* should be seen as it truly is with right discernment [*sammappaññāya*] thus: ‘This is not mine, this I am not, this is not my self.’
mn22:28.1: Seeing thus, *bhikkhus*, an instructed noble disciple becomes disenchanted with form [*rūpasmiṁ*], disenchanted with *vedanā*, disenchanted with *saññā*, disenchanted with *saṅkhāras*, disenchanted with *viññāṇa*.
mn22:29.1: Through disenchantment, he fades away; through fading away, he is liberated; when liberated, there is knowledge: ‘It is liberated.’
mn22:29.2: He understands: ‘Birth is destroyed, the holy life has been lived, what had to be done has been done; there is no more for this state of being.’
mn22:3.1: Then those *bhikkhus* approached the *bhikkhu* Ariṭṭha, formerly a vulture-trainer. Having approached, they said this to the *bhikkhu* Ariṭṭha, formerly a vulture-trainer:
mn22:3.10: sensual pleasures have been called by the *Bhagavā* like a piece of meat …
mn22:3.11: sensual pleasures have been called by the *Bhagavā* like a grass torch …
mn22:3.12: sensual pleasures have been called by the *Bhagavā* like a pit of coals …
mn22:3.13: sensual pleasures have been called by the *Bhagavā* like a dream …
mn22:3.14: sensual pleasures have been called by the *Bhagavā* like borrowed goods …
mn22:3.15: sensual pleasures have been called by the *Bhagavā* like fruits on a tree …
mn22:3.16: sensual pleasures have been called by the *Bhagavā* like a butcher’s block …
mn22:3.17: sensual pleasures have been called by the *Bhagavā* like a sword-stake …
mn22:3.18: sensual pleasures have been called by the *Bhagavā* like a snake’s head, much-*dukkha*, much-trouble, with more danger here.”
mn22:3.19: Yet even while being questioned, pressed, and spoken to by those *bhikkhus*, the *bhikkhu* Ariṭṭha, formerly a vulture-trainer, still spoke, clinging firmly, grasping, and insisting on that same harmful view:
mn22:3.2: “Is it true, friend Ariṭṭha, that such a harmful view has arisen in you:
mn22:3.20: “Indeed, friends, that is how I understand the *dhamma* taught by the *Bhagavā*: those things called obstructions by the *Bhagavā* are not enough to obstruct one who engages in them.”
mn22:3.21: When those *bhikkhus* were unable to detach the *bhikkhu* Ariṭṭha, formerly a vulture-trainer, from that harmful view, they approached the *Bhagavā*. Having approached, they paid homage to the *Bhagavā* and sat to one side. Sitting to one side, those *bhikkhus* said this to the *Bhagavā*:
mn22:3.22: “*Bhante*, a harmful view of this kind has arisen in a *bhikkhu* named Ariṭṭha, formerly a vulture-trainer:
mn22:3.23: ‘As I understand the *dhamma* taught by the *Bhagavā*, those things called obstructions by the *Bhagavā* are not enough to obstruct one who engages in them.’
mn22:3.25: ‘It seems that a harmful view of this kind has arisen in a *bhikkhu* named Ariṭṭha, formerly a vulture-trainer—
mn22:3.26: as I understand the *dhamma* taught by the *Bhagavā*, those things called obstructions by the *Bhagavā* are not enough to obstruct one who engages in them.’
mn22:3.27: Then, *bhante*, we approached the *bhikkhu* Ariṭṭha, formerly a vulture-trainer. Having approached, we said this to the *bhikkhu* Ariṭṭha, formerly a vulture-trainer:
mn22:3.28: ‘Is it true, friend Ariṭṭha, that such a harmful view has arisen in you—
mn22:3.29: as I understand the *dhamma* taught by the *Bhagavā*, those things called obstructions by the *Bhagavā* are not enough to obstruct one who engages in them?’
mn22:3.3: ‘As I understand the *dhamma* taught by the *Bhagavā*, those things called obstructions by the *Bhagavā* are not enough to obstruct one who engages in them’?”
mn22:3.30: When this was said, *bhante*, the *bhikkhu* Ariṭṭha, formerly a vulture-trainer, said this to us:
mn22:3.31: ‘Indeed, friends, that is how I understand the *dhamma* taught by the *Bhagavā*: those things called obstructions by the *Bhagavā* are not enough to obstruct one who engages in them.’
mn22:3.32: Then, *bhante*, wishing to detach the *bhikkhu* Ariṭṭha, formerly a vulture-trainer, from that harmful view, we questioned him, pressed him, and spoke to him:
mn22:3.33: ‘Do not say that, friend Ariṭṭha. Do not misrepresent the *Bhagavā*; for it is not *sādhu* to misrepresent the *Bhagavā*, and the *Bhagavā* would not speak thus.
mn22:3.34: In many ways, friend Ariṭṭha, obstructive things have been called obstructive by the *Bhagavā*, and they are enough to obstruct one who engages in them.
mn22:3.35: Sensual pleasures have been called by the *Bhagavā* little-satisfying, much-*dukkha*, much-trouble, with more danger here.
mn22:3.36: Sensual pleasures have been called by the *Bhagavā* like a skeleton …
mn22:3.37: sensual pleasures have been called by the *Bhagavā* like a snake’s head, much-*dukkha*, much-trouble, with more danger here.’
mn22:3.38: Yet even while being questioned, pressed, and spoken to by us, *bhante*, the *bhikkhu* Ariṭṭha, formerly a vulture-trainer, still spoke, clinging firmly, grasping, and insisting on that same harmful view:
mn22:3.39: ‘Indeed, friends, that is how I understand the *dhamma* taught by the *Bhagavā*: those things called obstructions by the *Bhagavā* are not enough to obstruct one who engages in them.’
mn22:3.4: “Indeed, friends, that is how I understand the *dhamma* taught by the *Bhagavā*: those things called obstructions by the *Bhagavā* are not enough to obstruct one who engages in them.”
mn22:3.5: Then those *bhikkhus*, wishing to detach the *bhikkhu* Ariṭṭha, formerly a vulture-trainer, from that harmful view, questioned him, pressed him, and spoke to him:
mn22:3.6: “Do not say that, friend Ariṭṭha. Do not misrepresent the *Bhagavā*; for it is not *sādhu* to misrepresent the *Bhagavā*, and the *Bhagavā* would not speak thus.
mn22:3.7: In many ways, friend Ariṭṭha, obstructive things have been called obstructive by the *Bhagavā*, and they are enough to obstruct one who engages in them.
mn22:3.8: Sensual pleasures have been called by the *Bhagavā* little-satisfying, much-*dukkha*, much-trouble, with more danger here.
mn22:3.9: Sensual pleasures have been called by the *Bhagavā* like a skeleton …
mn22:30.1: This is called, *bhikkhus*, a *bhikkhu* with the bar lifted, the trench filled in, the pillar pulled out, the bolt withdrawn, noble, with banner lowered, burden laid down, and detached.
mn22:31.1: And how, *bhikkhus*, is a *bhikkhu* one with the bar lifted?
mn22:31.2: Here, *bhikkhus*, a *bhikkhu* has abandoned blindness [*avijjā*], cut it off at the root, made it like a palm stump, made it non-existent, not subject to future arising.
mn22:31.3: Thus, *bhikkhus*, a *bhikkhu* is one with the bar lifted.
mn22:32.1: And how, *bhikkhus*, is a *bhikkhu* one with the trench filled in?
mn22:32.2: Here, *bhikkhus*, a *bhikkhu* has abandoned the cycle of birth in renewed existence, cut it off at the root, made it like a palm stump, made it non-existent, not subject to future arising.
mn22:32.3: Thus, *bhikkhus*, a *bhikkhu* is one with the trench filled in.
mn22:33.1: And how, *bhikkhus*, is a *bhikkhu* one with the pillar pulled out?
mn22:33.2: Here, *bhikkhus*, a *bhikkhu* has abandoned *taṇhā*, cut it off at the root, made it like a palm stump, made it non-existent, not subject to future arising.
mn22:33.3: Thus, *bhikkhus*, a *bhikkhu* is one with the pillar pulled out.
mn22:34.1: And how, *bhikkhus*, is a *bhikkhu* one with the bolt withdrawn?
mn22:34.2: Here, *bhikkhus*, a *bhikkhu* has abandoned the five lower fetters, cut them off at the root, made them like palm stumps, made them non-existent, not subject to future arising.
mn22:34.3: Thus, *bhikkhus*, a *bhikkhu* is one with the bolt withdrawn.
mn22:35.1: And how, *bhikkhus*, is a *bhikkhu* noble, with banner lowered, burden laid down, and detached?
mn22:35.2: Here, *bhikkhus*, a *bhikkhu* has abandoned the conceit ‘I am,’ cut it off at the root, made it like a palm stump, made it non-existent, not subject to future arising.
mn22:35.3: Thus, *bhikkhus*, a *bhikkhu* is noble, with banner lowered, burden laid down, and detached.
mn22:36.1: A *bhikkhu* whose mind [*cittaṁ*] is liberated in this way, *bhikkhus*, is not found by the *devatās* with Inda, Brahmā, and Pajāpati, when they search:
mn22:36.2: ‘This is what the Tathāgata’s *viññāṇa* depends on.’
mn22:36.4: Even in this very life, *bhikkhus*, I say the Tathāgata is untraceable.
mn22:37.1: When I speak and explain in this way, *bhikkhus*, some ascetics and brahmins misrepresent me with what is untrue, hollow, false, and unreal:
mn22:37.2: ‘The ascetic Gotama is an annihilationist; he proclaims the cutting off, destruction, and extermination of an existing being.’
mn22:37.3: Because I am not so, *bhikkhus*, because I do not speak so, those honorable ascetics and brahmins misrepresent me with what is untrue, hollow, false, and unreal:
mn22:37.4: ‘The ascetic Gotama is an annihilationist; he proclaims the cutting off, destruction, and extermination of an existing being.’
mn22:37.5: Formerly, *bhikkhus*, and now too, I make known only *dukkha* and the cessation of *dukkha*.
mn22:37.6: If there others abuse, insult, provoke, and harass the Tathāgata, there the Tathāgata has no resentment, no dissatisfaction, and no displeasure of mind.
mn22:38.1: If there others honor, respect, revere, and venerate the Tathāgata, there the Tathāgata has no delight, no gladness, and no elation of mind.
mn22:38.2: If there others honor, respect, revere, and venerate the Tathāgata, there it occurs to the Tathāgata:
mn22:38.3: ‘Such acts are being done toward this which was previously fully understood by me.’
mn22:39.1: Therefore, *bhikkhus*, if others abuse, insult, provoke, and harass you, there you should not create resentment, dissatisfaction, or displeasure of mind.
mn22:39.2: Therefore, *bhikkhus*, if others honor, respect, revere, and venerate you, there you should not create delight, gladness, or elation of mind.
mn22:39.3: Therefore, *bhikkhus*, if others honor, respect, revere, and venerate you, there it should occur to you:
mn22:39.4: ‘Such acts are being done toward this which was previously fully understood by me.’
mn22:4.1: When, *bhante*, we were unable to detach the *bhikkhu* Ariṭṭha, formerly a vulture-trainer, from that harmful view, then we reported this matter to the *Bhagavā*.”
mn22:40.1: Therefore, *bhikkhus*, abandon what is not yours;
mn22:40.2: when it is abandoned by you, that will be for your welfare and *sukha* for a long time.
mn22:41.11: When it is abandoned by you, that will be for your welfare and *sukha* for a long time.
mn22:41.13: if people carried away, burned, or did as they liked with the grass, sticks, branches, and leaves here in Jeta’s Grove,
mn22:41.15: ‘People are carrying us away, burning us, or doing as they like with us’?”
mn22:41.18: “For that is neither our self nor what belongs to our self, *bhante*.”
mn22:41.19: “So too, *bhikkhus*, abandon what is not yours;
mn22:41.2: Form [*rūpaṁ*], *bhikkhus*, is not yours; abandon it.
mn22:41.20: when it is abandoned by you, that will be for your welfare and *sukha* for a long time.
mn22:41.22: Form [*rūpaṁ*], *bhikkhus*, is not yours; abandon it.
mn22:41.23: When it is abandoned by you, that will be for your welfare and *sukha* for a long time.
mn22:41.28: When it is abandoned by you, that will be for your welfare and *sukha* for a long time.
mn22:41.3: When it is abandoned by you, that will be for your welfare and *sukha* for a long time.
mn22:41.5: When it is abandoned by you, that will be for your welfare and *sukha* for a long time.
mn22:41.7: When it is abandoned by you, that will be for your welfare and *sukha* for a long time.
mn22:41.9: When they are abandoned by you, that will be for your welfare and *sukha* for a long time.
mn22:42.1: Thus, *bhikkhus*, the *dhamma* has been well proclaimed by me: plain, open, revealed, with patchwork removed.
mn22:42.2: Thus, *bhikkhus*, in the *dhamma* well proclaimed by me — plain, open, revealed, with patchwork removed — those *bhikkhus* who are *arahants*, with *āsavas* destroyed [*khīṇāsavā*], who have lived the holy life, done what had to be done, laid down the burden, reached their own goal, with the fetters of existence entirely destroyed, completely liberated through final knowledge — there is no round for them to be designated.
mn22:43.1: Thus, *bhikkhus*, the *dhamma* has been well proclaimed by me: plain, open, revealed, with patchwork removed.
mn22:43.2: Thus, *bhikkhus*, in the *dhamma* well proclaimed by me — plain, open, revealed, with patchwork removed — those *bhikkhus* whose five lower fetters are abandoned are all spontaneously reborn, attain final *nibbāna* there, and are not subject to returning from that world.
mn22:44.1: Thus, *bhikkhus*, the *dhamma* has been well proclaimed by me: plain, open, revealed, with patchwork removed.
mn22:44.2: Thus, *bhikkhus*, in the *dhamma* well proclaimed by me — plain, open, revealed, with patchwork removed — those *bhikkhus* whose three fetters are abandoned and whose greed, hatred, and delusion are weakened are all once-returners; returning to this world only once, they will make an end of *dukkha*.
mn22:45.1: Thus, *bhikkhus*, the *dhamma* has been well proclaimed by me: plain, open, revealed, with patchwork removed.
mn22:45.2: Thus, *bhikkhus*, in the *dhamma* well proclaimed by me — plain, open, revealed, with patchwork removed — those *bhikkhus* whose three fetters are abandoned are all stream-enterers, not subject to downfall, fixed in destiny, bound for awakening.
mn22:46.1: Thus, *bhikkhus*, the *dhamma* has been well proclaimed by me: plain, open, revealed, with patchwork removed.
mn22:46.2: Thus, *bhikkhus*, in the *dhamma* well proclaimed by me — plain, open, revealed, with patchwork removed — those *bhikkhus* who are *dhamma*-followers and faith-followers are all bound for awakening.
mn22:47.1: Thus, *bhikkhus*, the *dhamma* has been well proclaimed by me: plain, open, revealed, with patchwork removed.
mn22:47.2: Thus, *bhikkhus*, in the *dhamma* well proclaimed by me — plain, open, revealed, with patchwork removed — those who have a mere measure of faith in me, a mere measure of love for me, are all bound for heaven.”
mn22:47.4: Satisfied, those *bhikkhus* rejoiced in the *Bhagavā*’s words.
mn22:47.5: The Simile of the Snake is finished, the second.
mn22:5.2: “Come, *bhikkhu*, in my name summon the *bhikkhu* Ariṭṭha, formerly a vulture-trainer:
mn22:5.4: “Yes, *bhante*,” that *bhikkhu* replied to the *Bhagavā*. He approached the *bhikkhu* Ariṭṭha, formerly a vulture-trainer. Having approached, he said this to the *bhikkhu* Ariṭṭha, formerly a vulture-trainer:
mn22:5.6: “Yes, friend,” the *bhikkhu* Ariṭṭha, formerly a vulture-trainer, replied to that *bhikkhu*. He approached the *Bhagavā*. Having approached, he paid homage to the *Bhagavā* and sat to one side. As the *bhikkhu* Ariṭṭha, formerly a vulture-trainer, sat to one side, the *Bhagavā* said this to him:
mn22:5.7: “Is it true, Ariṭṭha, that such a harmful view has arisen in you:
mn22:5.8: ‘As I understand the *dhamma* taught by the *Bhagavā*, those things called obstructions by the *Bhagavā* are not enough to obstruct one who engages in them’?”
mn22:5.9: “Indeed, *bhante*, that is how I understand the *dhamma* taught by the *Bhagavā*: ‘Those things called obstructions by the *Bhagavā* are not enough to obstruct one who engages in them.’”
mn22:6.1: “To whom, you hollow man, have you ever understood me to teach the *dhamma* in that way?
mn22:6.10: sensual pleasures have been called by me like fruits on a tree …
mn22:6.11: sensual pleasures have been called by me like a butcher’s block …
mn22:6.12: sensual pleasures have been called by me like a sword-stake …
mn22:6.13: sensual pleasures have been called by me like a snake’s head, much-*dukkha*, much-trouble, with more danger here.
mn22:6.14: Yet you, hollow man, by your own bad grasp, misrepresent us, dig yourself up, and generate much demerit.
mn22:6.15: For this will be for your harm and *dukkha* for a long time.”
mn22:6.2: Hollow man, have I not in many ways called obstructive things obstructive? And they are enough to obstruct one who engages in them.
mn22:6.3: Sensual pleasures have been called by me little-satisfying, much-*dukkha*, much-trouble, with more danger here.
mn22:6.4: Sensual pleasures have been called by me like a skeleton …
mn22:6.5: sensual pleasures have been called by me like a piece of meat …
mn22:6.6: sensual pleasures have been called by me like a grass torch …
mn22:6.7: sensual pleasures have been called by me like a pit of coals …
mn22:6.8: sensual pleasures have been called by me like a dream …
mn22:6.9: sensual pleasures have been called by me like borrowed goods …
mn22:7.3: has this *bhikkhu* Ariṭṭha, formerly a vulture-trainer, generated even a spark in this *dhamma* and *vinaya* [*dhammavinaye*]?”
mn22:7.6: When this was said, the *bhikkhu* Ariṭṭha, formerly a vulture-trainer, sat silent, dismayed, shoulders drooping, head down, brooding, at a loss for words.
mn22:7.7: Then the *Bhagavā*, knowing that the *bhikkhu* Ariṭṭha, formerly a vulture-trainer, was silent, dismayed, shoulders drooping, head down, brooding, at a loss for words, said this to the *bhikkhu* Ariṭṭha, formerly a vulture-trainer:
mn22:7.8: “You will be known, hollow man, by this harmful view of your own.
mn22:8.10: For in many ways, *bhikkhus*, obstructive things have been called by me, and they are enough to obstruct one who engages in them.
mn22:8.11: Sensual pleasures have been called by me little-satisfying, much-*dukkha*, much-trouble, with more danger here.
mn22:8.12: Sensual pleasures have been called by me like a skeleton …
mn22:8.13: sensual pleasures have been called by me like a snake’s head, much-*dukkha*, much-trouble, with more danger here.
mn22:8.14: Yet this *bhikkhu* Ariṭṭha, formerly a vulture-trainer, by his own bad grasp, misrepresents us, digs himself up, and generates much demerit.
mn22:8.15: For this will be for that hollow man’s harm and *dukkha* for a long time.
mn22:8.2: “Do you too, *bhikkhus*, understand the *dhamma* taught by me in this way, as this *bhikkhu* Ariṭṭha, formerly a vulture-trainer, misrepresents us by his own bad grasp, digs himself up, and generates much demerit?”
mn22:8.4: For in many ways, *bhante*, obstructive things have been called obstructive by the *Bhagavā*;
mn22:8.5: and they are enough to obstruct one who engages in them.
mn22:8.6: Sensual pleasures have been called by the *Bhagavā* little-satisfying, much-*dukkha*, much-trouble, with more danger here.
mn22:8.7: Sensual pleasures have been called by the *Bhagavā* like a skeleton …
mn22:8.8: sensual pleasures have been called by the *Bhagavā* like a snake’s head, much-*dukkha*, much-trouble, with more danger here.”
mn22:8.9: “*Sādhu*, *sādhu*, *bhikkhus*. It is *sādhu*, *bhikkhus*, that you understand the *dhamma* taught by me in this way.
mn22:9.1: That one could engage in sensual pleasures apart from sensual desires, apart from perceptions of sensuality, apart from thoughts [*vitakkehi*] of sensuality — such a possibility does not exist.
